antiPatterns: Supporting further predator population growth while nutrients are critically low.
Ignoring the disparity between active nutrients (9) and the nutrient buffer (100).
Focusing on population expansion traits while the ecological network is failing to sustain existing density.

directions.acceptanceSignal: Fox population growth rate slows down significantly.
directions.avoid: Traits that improve predator efficiency or reproductive rate.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Stabilization or decrease in fox population growth rate.
directions.label: A
directions.title: Limit Fox Metabolic Over-Exploitation
directions.why: The current population size is over-taxing the ecosystem.

directions.acceptanceSignal: Active nutrient levels increase steadily toward 20+.
directions.avoid: Traits that increase consumer consumption rates.
directions.label: B
directions.title: Accelerate Nutrient Buffer Release
directions.why: The buffer (100) is full while nutrients are low (9); the system bottleneck is conversion.

directions.acceptanceSignal: Improved nutrient regeneration rate observed in population snapshots.
directions.avoid: Focusing on fungal biomass growth at the expense of decomposition rate.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Increased rate of energy conversion into the nutrient pool.
directions.label: C
directions.title: Enhance Fungal Decomposition Efficiency
directions.why: Fungal foundation needs better throughput to sustain the energy requirements of the current predator density.

directions.avoid: Traits that heavily increase predation pressure on beetles.
directions.label: D
directions.title: Beetle Population Stabilization
directions.why: Beetle population (140) is declining, threatening the primary prey base for the now-massive fox population.

results.evidence: Nutrient levels remain low (9); the buffer (100) is under-utilized despite the massive growth in consumer population.
results.label: A

results.evidence: Fungal/Root networks remain stable, but nutrient cycling throughput is insufficient for current population levels.
results.label: B

results.evidence: Fox population exploded from 1634 to 3434, the opposite of metabolic stability.
results.label: C

results.evidence: Beetle population declined slightly from 181 to 140, indicating stress in the prey population.
results.label: D

review.summary: Fox population grew explosively (doubled from 1634 to 3434), surpassing all stability goals. Nutrient levels improved slightly (3 to 9) but remain critically low. Beetle population declined from 181 to 140, indicating instability in the prey base.

thesis: The fox population has reached an unsustainable level (3434) relative to the critically low nutrient pool (9). To prevent a catastrophic system crash, we must shift focus from supporting expansion to enforcing metabolic constraint on predators, accelerating the release of the abundant nutrient buffer (100) into the active pool, and bolstering the foundational decomposer (Fungus) network.
